data.title: Fitch Affirms IBM's IDR at 'A-'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Chicago-21 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed International Business Machines' (IBM) Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'A-'. The Rating Outlook is Stable. Fitch has also affirmed the rating for IBM's unsecured notes at 'A-'. In addition, Fitch has affirmed IBM's Short-Term IDR and commercial paper (CP) ratings at 'F1'.

IBM's ratings are supported by its solid market position in hybrid cloud and AI, including digital transformations. Acquisitions and divestitures have aligned its three major business units with this strategy to deliver such capabilities to its customers. Fitch expects IBM to continue acquisitions funded by internally generated cash.

Strong FCF generation, evidenced by the core cash flow from operations (CFO) minus capital expenditure (capex) to debt ratio, is consistent with the rating category. The ratings are constrained by relatively high core EBITDA leverage. Fitch forecasts gradual deleveraging via EBITDA growth while keeping total debt levels stable. Deviations from these expectations could trigger rating actions.

Key Rating Drivers

Resilient Operating Profile: Fitch forecasts IBM to grow its revenue in the low to mid-single digits while maintaining stable margins supported by the durability in its revenue structure. Fitch believes the alignment across IBM's strategy and operations enhance the resiliency and predictability of its business segments, particularly with over 70% of total revenue being derived from software and consulting services which Fitch views as highly synergistic.

IBM's infrastructure segment also contributes to the overall hybrid cloud strategy with its differentiated position as the primary mainframe system supplier. The recently launched z17 should provide a near-term boost to revenue growth. Across the three segments, IBM is in a unique position to offer products and services supporting mainframe, multi-cloud, infrastructure software, and implementation services.

Solid Position for Complex IT: IBM has reoriented itself after the Red Hat acquisition with a focus on hybrid cloud and AI technologies and services, enabling digital transformations. The software segment provides the infrastructure capabilities anchored by Red Hat Enterprise Linux and the AI capabilities of watsonx. The consulting segment provides implementation support for technology, applications, and business transformation. The infrastructure segment provides major subsystems for hybrid cloud environments.

Secular Tailwind Supporting Growth: Digital transformation and hybrid cloud remain important growth drivers for the technology industry, as businesses and government agencies digitalize workflow to increase efficiency. AI is increasing demand spanning data structure to enterprise AI implementations. IBM's software and consulting businesses are anchored around these growth areas and should benefit from the continuing demand growth. IBM's z17 system capabilities include cyber-resilient security, embedded AI at scale and cloud-native development for hybrid cloud.

Significant Revenue Visibility: IBM has recently seen a reacceleration in consulting revenue in 3Q25, following a period of softness in 1H25. Fitch expects this improvement to continue into 2026. Software revenue remains strong, with profitability benefiting from operating leverage. Over 40% of IBM's total revenue is derived from its software segment with 80% recurring. While consulting revenue visibility is generally less certain than software, IBM's consulting's focus on efficiency-enhancing areas and complementary nature to IBM software products could offer greater resilience.

Cyclical Infrastructure Segment: IBM's infrastructure segment generates less than 25% of total revenue and is expected to decline throughout Fitch's forecast period. The segment consists of its IBM Z mainframe systems and other infrastructure products and support. While Fitch expects the utility of mainframe systems to remain static as new workloads are deployed in the cloud environment, mainframe systems remain a critical infrastructure component in the hybrid cloud environment. Fitch believes legacy mission critical mainframe workloads will continue to reside on mainframe systems for the foreseeable future.

Ample Financial Flexibility for Acquisitions: Acquisitions remain a core growth strategy to strengthen IBM's positions within its core product segments. The company has also divested non-core businesses, including Kyndryl, IBM Watson Health, and The Weather Company. Fitch believes acquisitions and divestitures have reshaped IBM and sharpened its focus on hybrid cloud and AI strategy. Fitch anticipates these transactions will continue and that IBM would primarily use internally generated cash for acquisitions.

Strong FCF Generation: Fitch estimates IBM will generate annual pre-dividend FCF near $14 billion through the forecast period. This results in core (CFO-capex)/debt ratios currently near 30% and rising. The strong FCF generation provides IBM with ample financial flexibility for investments to further strengthen its capabilities around hybrid cloud and AI.

Deleveraging Trajectory with Stable Debt: Fitch assumes IBM will maintain its current level of outstanding debt throughout the forecast period, consistent with its capital structure since the Kyndryl divestiture in 2021. Deviations could result in rating actions. Fitch expects deleveraging through EBITDA growth without material reduction in debt. Fitch does not expect meaningful changes to IBM's shareholder return policies. Acquisitions are likely to be funded with internally generated cash. Fitch estimates IBM's core leverage to decline to 2.3x-2.4x by 2026-2027 from3.0x at YE 2023.

Peer Analysis

IBM has transformed its business model in recent years with a clear focus around digital transformation, hybrid cloud, and AI. In Fitch's view, the three main operating segments operate with a consistent focus optimizing strategic synergies. The company has solidified its software position within hybrid cloud infrastructure and application software anchored by Red Hat hybrid cloud platform with consulting complementing the software offerings.

Among IBM's peer groups are technology companies in the 'A' to 'BBB' rating categories. IBM has significantly larger revenue and EBITDA scales and revenue diversity than Cadence Design Systems Inc. (A-/Stable), KLA Corporation (A/Stable) and TE Connectivity (A-/Stable). IBM's core EBITDA is more than 2x that of KLA Corporation and approximately 10x that of Cadence Design Systems. However, IBM's core EBITDA leverage in the mid-2x range is higher than all three 'A' category peers that have EBITDA leverage in the range of 0.4x-1.3x, and the three peers have stronger (CFO-capex)/debt ratios than IBM. IBM also has greater end-market diversification than these peers.

In the 'BBB+' and 'BBB' rating categories, IBM compares favorably against Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (BBB+/Stable), HP Inc. (BBB+/Stable) and Oracle (BBB/Stable). IBM has similar EBITDA leverage as both Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company and HP Inc. However, IBM's core EBITDA is nearly 3x larger with more revenue diversification and visibility. IBM has lower EBITDA than Oracle and core EBITDA leverage is substantially below that of Oracle's. When comparing (CFO-capex)/debt ratios, IBM is strongly positioned against these 'BBB' category peers.

Key Assumptions

--Organic revenue growth ranging from low single digits to high single digits due to product mix variations;

--EBITDA margins remaining stable in the mid- to high 20s;

--Capex remaining stable at 2% of revenue;

--Acquisitions averaging $5.5 billion to $6.0 billion annually;

--Dividend growth in line with revenue growth;

--Total outstanding debt remaining stable (excluding pre-funding debt for maturities).

RATING SENSITIVITIES

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--Structurally lower revenue or EBITDA from material share losses resulting in degradation in FCF generation;

--Sustained core EBITDA leverage above 2.25x and (CFO-capex)/debt below 25% potentially due to more aggressive capital structure that deviates from Fitch's expectations.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--Sufficient capacity and financial flexibility to execute M&A strategy and capital allocation priorities enabling the company to continuously upgrade technology capabilities and maintain competitiveness without significant degradation in credit metrics;

--Publicly articulated financial framework or a demonstrated record of maintaining core EBITDA leverage under 1.5x and (CFO-capex)/debt above 30% combined with operating performance that is in line with Fitch's current expectations.

Liquidity and Debt Structure

As of Sept. 30, 2025, IBM had $11.569 billion of cash and cash equivalents and $3.286 billion of marketable securities on the balance sheet. Fitch's expectation of approximately $14 billion in annual pre-dividend FCF and over $6 billion in annual post-dividend FCF also supports liquidity. IBM also has access to an additional $10 billion under its CP facility and revolving credit facility.

Fitch estimates IBM to have ample liquidity to support operating activities, acquisitions and servicing of debt obligations without the need for external capital through 2026. Furthermore, Fitch assumes IBM will continue to maintain its total debt at or below current levels beyond 2026.

IBM has $6.436 billion of debt due in 2026 and $6.744 billion of debt due in 2027. The remaining $49.101 billion of debt matures after 2027.

As part of its captive finance adjustment, Fitch has calculated an allowable debt to equity ratio of 5.0x for the IBM Financing Segment. Fitch has assumed that IBM makes a theoretical $1.077 billion equity injection into the IBM Financing Segment, funded with balance sheet cash. Fitch has included the adjustment in IBM's not readily available cash.

Issuer Profile

IBM is a global enterprise software, IT services, and hardware provider. The company's products and services focus on hybrid cloud adoption and AI offerings.

data.title: Exxon freezes plans for major hydrogen plant amid weak customer demand
data.description:
By Sheila Dang

Exxon Mobil has paused plans to build what would be one of the world's largest hydrogen production facilities due to weak customer demand, CEO Darren Woods told Reuters in an interview on Friday.

The suspension of the project, which had already experienced delays, reflects a wider slowdown in efforts by traditional oil and gas firms to transition to cleaner energy sources as many of the initiatives struggle to turn a profit.

U.S. President Donald Trump had also yanked funding for green initiatives to focus on fossil fuels.

Oil major Exxon announced plans in 2022 to build the plant at its refining and chemical complex in Baytown, Texas, with a goal of producing 1 billion cubic feet per day of so-called blue hydrogen, a clean fuel that produces water when burned.

Potential customers have stayed on the sidelines due to the higher cost of using hydrogen, Woods said, adding that an industrial slowdown and economic uncertainty in Europe have further crimped demand.

"There's been a continued challenge to establish committed customers who are willing to provide contracts for off-take," Woods said.

Hydrogen can be produced from natural gas and Exxon planned to capture the carbon dioxide generated from the process and store it underground. That carries a higher cost than hydrogen produced without containing the carbon dioxide, and not enough customers are currently willing to pay the premium, Woods said.

Exxon and its partners on the facility, including Abu Dhabi National Oil Company, have invested about $500 million so far into the project that is estimated to cost several billion dollars, he said, declining to give a precise figure.

Exxon can restart the project when there is enough market demand, Woods added, though it is unclear when that could be.

"We remain convinced that one, it will be needed, and two, this will be an advantaged project to meet that need," Woods said.

data.title: Tesla Stock Is Having a Terrible November. Who That's Hitting Hardest. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

The market is struggling to shake fears that the AI trade has gone too far. That's hurt retail investors recently.

Tesla stock didn't get a boost from stronger-than-expected earnings posted by Nvidia on Wednesday evening. That was a surprise. Initially, investors reacted with relief to Nvidia's earnings.

Nvidia's stock traded as high as $196 on Thursday before declining to $180.64, down 3.2%. Tesla shares traded as high as $428.94 before closing at $395.04, down 2.2%.

Tesla stock fell 1% on Friday, closing at $391.09, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average gained 1% and 1.1%, respectively.

The recent declines appear to have hurt smaller so-called retail investors the most, instead of institutional money managers running mutual funds and the like.

This week, "retail investors rotated out of Health Care, Industrials, and Staples, funneling funds into Consumer Discretionary (driven by Tesla) and Communications ( Netflix, Alphabet)," wrote J.P. Morgan strategist Arun Jain in a recent report. They were also buying Nvidia stock "every day this week, ahead of its earnings," he noted.

Jain tracks what retail investors are buying and selling. It's one piece of information that helps explain investor sentiment. Retail investors have been "buying the dip" this year, he added, piling into winners after declines.

It's been a winning strategy so far in 2025, but it hasn't helped recently, especially after Thursday's big reversal. Thursday trading left Tesla stock down about 13% month to date and Nvidia stock off about 11%.

Retail investors can console themselves by remembering that Nvidia stock was still up 35% year to date, through Thursday trading. Tesla stock was down about 2% year to date, not great, but it was up about 84% from the 52-week low reached in April.

Why Nvidia and Tesla shares are treated similarly by retail investors isn't hard to figure out. They are both Magnificent Seven stocks and part of the AI trade driving the stock market.

Of course, they are a little different. Nvidia makes the hardware and software enabling AI computing. Its shares trade for about 25 times estimated 2026 earnings, according to FactSet. Hyperscalers such as Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta Platforms are building data centers for AI computing. They trade for an average of about 25 times earnings.

Tesla stock trades for roughly 180 times estimated 2026 earnings. It's building AI applications with AI computing. Earnings from its AI-trained self-driving cars and robots haven't materialized yet.

data.title: How China Built Rare-Earth Dominance, and How the U.S. Can Fight Back — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

The domestic rare-earth industry has gone from zero to hero in the blink of an eye this year, as the U.S. suddenly realized that ceding a small upstream commodity industry to China threatened America's ability to make everything from cars to drones to AI-trained robots.

Understanding just how China came to monopolize the rare-earth industry can help investors anticipate what's coming next, making it easier to invest in the critical — and exceedingly volatile — sector.

"While China's industry is highly skilled and highly capable, its dominant position is both enabled and hardened by subsidies, price manipulation, and scale," said Matthew Sloustcher, MP Materials' executive vice president of corporate affairs, in testimony to the House Select Committee on China earlier this week.

"In the early years of our business, we produced mineral concentrate at Mountain Pass [mine in California] that could only be sold into China, the sole market with the refining capacity to process it."

MP, of course, didn't want to sell intermediate products to China forever, so it reinvested its revenue into refining capabilities. It was an untenable strategy amid Chinese dominance, however.

"But as we prepared to launch, neodymium-praseodymium prices, [a] key commodity, collapsed to levels below even China's lowest production costs," explained Sloustcher. "That predatory pricing destroyed incentives to invest, and it kept capital scarce."

It's a textbook strategy to grow market share and keep competitors out: Drive prices lower, so other players can't build up their own business. It worked spectacularly. In 1985, the U.S. mined almost half of the world's rare-earth minerals, while China mined 0%. By 2010, China mined 70% and the U.S. mined 0%. China also came to dominate refining, with an estimated 85% of global capacity.

The shifting industry structure didn't go unnoticed. The Defense Department has worked for the better part of a decade to reduce dependence on potential foreign adversaries. It took a huge step in July, announcing a deal with MP that included an equity stake, a price floor for rare-earth materials, and a guaranteed customer for the rare-earth magnet capacity that MP is building.

"In a short period of time, we'll have everything we need for ourselves, " President Donald Trump told 60 Minutes in early November.

Still, it will take a while for China's influence to diminish. Beijing's October threats to restrict rare-earth exports — a position it later backed off from — injected more volatility into the sector.

Coming into Friday trading, MP stock was down 24% over the past month. Shares of aspiring rare-earth miners USA Rare Earth and Ramaco Resources were down an incredible 54% and 60%, respectively.

Volatility takes its own toll: It is synonymous with risk for investors. Higher-risk investments require higher returns, raising the cost of capital for anyone looking to start a new business.

Recent trading is a warning that the U.S. shouldn't declare victory in the rare-earth war just yet.

"Where appropriate, the federal government should deploy tools that neutralize predatory pricing," while easing access to project permits and working with allies, added Sloustcher. "Finally, the United States should incentivize and reward American companies that source U.S.-produced critical minerals and derivative products."

MP and other rare-earth producers need domestic customers, too. China isn't only the largest producer, it's the largest customer for rare-earth products, which end up in everything from iPhones to electric vehicles to fighter jets.

China might have overplayed its hand. The U.S. is intent on building a domestic rare-earth supply chain, and companies including Apple and General Motors have made deals to secure a domestic supply of such minerals.

A lot of stakeholders have an interest in the success of the domestic rare-earth industry. It's one reason that, despite recent declines, MP shares were still up 263% year to date through Thursday trading.

MP shares were up 0.6% in early trading on Friday at $57, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were up about 0.5%. Ramaco shares were down 0.7%. USA Rare Earth stock was off 0.3%.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: BOJ nearing decision to raise rates, board member Masu says -Nikkei
data.description:
The Bank of Japan is "nearing" a decision to raise interest rates and will not wait until after next year's spring wage negotiations end, its board member Kazuyuki Masu was quoted in the Nikkei newspaper as saying.

"I can't say what month it'll be, but in terms of distance, we're close," Masu said on the timing of the central bank's next rate hike, according to the Nikkei, which interviewed him on Thursday.

The central bank must raise interest rates as it would be "not good" for inflation-adjusted, real borrowing costs to remain deeply negative, he was quoted as saying.

Japan's policy interest rate, at 0.5%, is lower than what is considered neutral to the economy, which is another reason for hiking rates, he added.

"Looking at the economic and price situation, I do think the environment is favourable for raising interest rates. This is not monetary tightening, but simply part of the normalisation process," Masu said, according to the Nikkei.

data.title: Fitch Rates Louisiana's Gas Tax Revenue Bonds at 'AA-'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-21 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has assigned a 'AA-' rating to the following state of Louisiana bonds:

--$116.59 million gasoline and fuels tax second lien revenue refunding bonds, 2025 Series C;

--$116.59 million gasoline and fuels tax second lien revenue refunding bonds, 2025 Series C (bank bonds).

The bonds are scheduled to be offered on or about December 9 via competitive sale. Par amounts and timing are preliminary and subject to change.

In addition, Fitch has affirmed the following ratings on the state of Louisiana at 'AA-':

--First lien gasoline and fuels tax (gas tax) revenue bonds;

--Second lien gas tax revenue bonds.

Proceeds of the 2025 series C bonds will be used to refund the state's gasoline and fuels tax second lien revenue refunding bonds, 2022 series A and pay costs of issuance. Based on a review of the terms governing the bank bonds, it is Fitch's opinion that the incremental risk associated with bank bonds does not have a material effect on the long-term credit rating of the gasoline and fuels tax bonds.

The Rating Outlook is Stable.

The 'AA-' ratings on Louisiana's first and second lien gasoline tax revenue bonds incorporate the robust resilience of both liens to volatility in the full pledged revenue stream (Act 16 and existing taxes) through economic downturns. Prospects for revenue growth are modest and reflect ongoing national trends of weakening growth in fuel consumption, as well as Fitch's expectation for slow economic growth in the state, relative to the nation. With no authorization or plans for additional new money borrowing, pledged revenues should support ample coverage of outstanding obligations, despite the escalating debt service schedule.

Dedicated Tax Security

The first and second lien gas tax revenue bonds are special and limited obligations of the state of Louisiana payable from pledged gasoline and fuels taxes. Total pledged revenues for both liens include both Act 16 taxes (4 cents) and existing taxes (16 cents). The second lien bonds are backed from the same revenue stream as the first lien bonds, but on a subordinate basis.

Dedicated Tax Key Rating Drivers

Growth Prospects for Revenues - 'bbb'

Generally Stable Revenue Stream with Limited Growth Potential: The bonds are backed by gasoline and motor and special fuels tax revenues that have shown relative stability over time. Given ongoing improvements in vehicle fuel economy and gradual transition to electric vehicles, as well as the state's own modest economic growth prospects, Fitch believes the revenue stream has limited growth potential, consistent with a 'bbb' assessment.

Sensitivity and Resilience - 'aaa'

Resilient Security: The pledged revenue stream can absorb a significant decline and still provide strong coverage of debt service under the current structure, supporting a 'aaa' assessment. This strength is bolstered by the closure of the first lien and current lack of authorization for additional leverage on the second lien.

Exposure To Related Government

Linkage to State's IDR: Fitch caps the rating on the gasoline and fuels tax bonds at the state's Issuer Default Rating (IDR) and GO bond rating (AA-/Stable), given the flow of non-Act 16 taxes (existing taxes) through the state's bond security and redemption fund for state GO debt service, before these tax revenues are available for the gasoline and fuels tax bonds.

Dedicated Tax Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

-- Greater-than-anticipated revenue declines or unexpected new money issuance that weakens the resilience of the bonds' structure, as evidenced by lower coverage of maximum leverage closer to 1.5x, versus the fiscal 2025 coverage of 4.1x for the first lien and 3.2x for the second lien.

-- Negative rating action on the state's IDR, given the rating cap.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

-- Positive rating action on the state's IDR, given the rating cap, although the rating would not necessarily move in lockstep with the IDR.

-- A sustained improvement in the growth prospects for gasoline and fuels tax revenues to at least the level of Fitch's long-term expectations for national inflation.

Dedicated Tax Key Rating Drivers

Growth Prospects for Revenues - 'bbb'

Sensitivity and Resilience - 'aaa'

PROFILE

Louisiana's economy is broad but with a higher concentration in natural resources than the rest of the nation, particularly as a manufacturer of oil and natural gas-derived products. Louisiana's economic growth continues to lag the nation and other states in terms of overall level and trends. Tourism and trade remain important to the state economy, and the port system is among the largest in the world.

In cases where Fitch does not provide ESG relevance scores in connection with the credit rating of a transaction, program, instrument or issuer, Fitch will disclose any ESG factor that is a key rating driver in the key rating drivers section of the relevant rating action commentary. data.title: Fitch Downgrades Rocky Mount, NC IDR and Sales Tax Bonds to 'AA-'; Outlook Negative
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-21 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has downgraded Rocky Mount, NC's Issuer Default Rating (IDR) and Sales Tax Bonds to 'AA-' from 'AA' on financial resilience concerns. The Rating Outlook is Negative.

The downgrade to 'AA-' and Negative Outlook reflect a recent budgetary imbalance, including the use of nonrecurring resources for operational expenditure, which could result in a lower financial resilience assessment if not resolved. The fiscal 2024 deficit as well as expected deficits in fiscal 2025 and 2026 are consequences of expenditure outpacing revenue, primarily due to personnel expenditure resulting from salary increases and excessive hiring. The city has taken measures to address this imbalance, including reductions in staffing through layoffs and hiring freezes, as well as a tax rate increases for fiscal 2026. However, the full impact of these policies has yet to be realized this year, and the fiscal 2025 reserve draw will not be known until the fiscal 2025 audit is published, which is expected in December.

Due to the recent material draw on fund balance for operations, the rating includes a negative notch under the additional analytical factor (AAF). The negative notch could be removed if the city resolves its structural imbalance. Conversely, if the city fails to make progress toward returning to balanced operations as planned, an additional negative notch could be applied, resulting in a downgrade.

The 'AA-' ratings reflect the city's weakest economic- and demographic-level metrics and population trend balanced against a 'aaa' financial resilience assessment, assuming reserves remain above 7.5% of spending, and a relatively low long-term liability burden.

The Sales Tax Bonds rating downgrade to 'AA-' and the Negative Outlook reflect the city's IDR and Outlook, since the bond rating is capped at the city's IDR because the dedicated revenue stream lacks insulation and could be used for general operations in a bankruptcy filing. Underlying growth prospects are assessed at 'aaa' because the revenue stream's 10-year CAGR surpasses national GDP growth. The sensitivity of the revenue stream is assessed at 'aaa' based on the high maximum annual debt service (MADS) coverage and remote risk of additional leverage.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

IDR:

--Failure to use available policy measures as needed to reduce the structural deficit, leading to continued material reliance on one-time measures to balance budgets;

-- Expected or actual available reserves sustained below 7.5% of spending, resulting in 'aa' or lower financial resilience assessment;

--An increase of approximately 5% in long-term liabilities, assuming current levels of personal income, governmental revenue and spending.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

Sales Tax Bonds:

--A downgrade of the city's IDR;

--A decline in the city's sales tax revenue growth to below the rate of inflation;

--A sustained decline in debt service coverage to approximately 1.2X MADS.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

IDR:

--The Rating Outlook could be revised to 'Stable' if the city demonstrates stable operations with limited to no use of one-time resources to support operations;

--A substantial improvement in economic and demographic metrics, including population trend and economic concentration.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

Sales Tax Bonds:

--An upgrade of the city's IDR or a revision of the city's Outlook to "Stable"

SECURITY

The Sales Tax Revenue bonds are special obligations of the city, payable solely from the trust estate pledged under the indenture, which includes the pledged funds.

Fitch’s Local Government Rating Model

The Local Government Rating Model generates Model Implied Ratings, which communicate the issuer's credit quality relative to Fitch's local government rating portfolio. (The Model Implied Rating will be the Issuer Default Rating except in certain circumstances explained in the applicable criteria.) The Model Implied Rating is expressed via a numerical value calibrated to Fitch's long-term rating scale that ranges from 10.0 or higher (AAA), 9.0 (AA+), 8.0 (AA), and so forth down to 1.0 (BBB- and below).

Model Implied Ratings reflect the combination of issuer-specific metrics and assessments to generate a Metric Profile and a structured framework to account for Additional Analytical Factors not captured in the Metric Profile that can either mitigate or exacerbate credit risks. Additional Analytical Factors are reflected in notching from the Metric Profile and are capped at +/-3 notches.

Ratings Headroom & Positioning

Rocky Mount Model Implied Rating: 'AA-' (Numerical Value: 7.03)

-- Metric Profile: 'AA' (Numerical Value: 8.03)

-- Net Additional Analytical Factor Notching: -1.0

Individual Additional Analytical Notching Factors:

-- Non-Recurring Support or Spending Deferrals: -1.0

Rocky Mount's Model Implied Rating is 'AA-'. The associated numerical value of 7.03 is at the lower end of the 7.0 to 8.0 range for a 'AA-' rating. The application of a -1 notch Additional Analytical Factor for Non-Recurring Support or Spending Deferrals brings the MIR to 7.03, supporting the 'AA-' rating.

Key Rating Drivers Financial Profile

Financial Resilience - 'aaa'

Rocky Mount's financial resilience is driven by the combination of its 'High' revenue control assessment and 'High' expenditure control assessment, culminating in a 'Ample' budgetary flexibility assessment.

-- Revenue control assessment: High

-- Expenditure control assessment: High

-- Budgetary flexibility assessment: Ample

-- Minimum fund balance for current financial resilience assessment: >=7.5%

-- Current year fund balance to expenditure ratio: 20.3% (2024)

-- Lowest fund balance to expenditure ratio for the fiscal-year period 2020-2024: 20.3% (2024)

Revenue Volatility - 'Strongest'

Rocky Mount's weakest historic three-year revenue performance is neutral to the Model Implied Rating.

The revenue volatility metric is an estimate of potential revenue volatility based on the issuer's historical experience relative to the median for the Fitch-rated local government portfolio. The metric helps to differentiate issuers by the scale of revenue loss that would have to be addressed through revenue raising, cost controls or utilization of reserves through economic cycles.

-- Lowest three-year revenue performance (based on revenue dating back to 2005): 2.5% increase for the three-year period ending fiscal 2015

-- Median issuer decline: -4.3% (2024)

Financial Profile Additional Analytical Factors and Notching: -1.0 notch (for Non-Recurring Support or Spending Deferrals)

The city's fiscal 2024 audit and fiscal 2025 projections indicate significant use of fund balance to support operational expenditures, resulting in a -1 notch to the city's MIR. Should the reliance on nonrecurring funding be sustained or increased, an additional negative notch could be applied.

Demographic and Economic Strength

Population Trend - 'Weakest'

Based on the median of 10-year annual percentage change in population, Rocky Mount's population trend is assessed as 'Weakest'.

Population trend: -0.3% Analyst Input (4th percentile) (vs. -0.6% 2023 median of 10-year annual percentage change in population)

Unemployment, Educational Attainment and MHI Level - 'Weakest'

The overall strength of Rocky Mount's demographic- and economic-level indicators (unemployment rate, educational attainment, median household income [MHI]) in 2024 are assessed as 'Weakest' on a composite basis, performing at the 18th percentile of Fitch's local government rating portfolio. This is due to relatively weak education attainment levels, median-issuer indexed adjusted MHI and unemployment rate.

-- Unemployment rate as a percentage of national rate: 142.5% 2024 (15th percentile), relative to the national rate of 4.0%

-- Percent of population with a bachelor's degree or higher: 21.5% (2023) (25th percentile)

-- MHI as a percent of the portfolio median: 75.4% (2023) (13th percentile)

Economic Concentration and Population Size - 'Midrange'

Rocky Mount's population in 2023 was of sufficient size to qualify for Fitch's highest overall size category but this is offset by the somewhat concentrated economy.

The composite metric acts asymmetrically, with most issuers (above the 15th percentile for each metric) sufficiently diversified to minimize risks associated with small population and economic concentration. Downward effects of the metric on the Metric Profile are most pronounced for the least economically diverse issuers (in the 5th percentile for the metric or lower). The economic concentration percentage shown below is defined as the sum of the absolute deviation of the percentage of personal income by major economic sectors relative to the U.S. distribution.

-- Population size: 54,541 Analyst Input (above the 15th percentile) (vs. 54,388 2023 Actual)

-- Economic concentration: 52.7% Analyst Input (from 10th to 15th percentile) (vs. 42.0% 2024 Actual)

Analyst Inputs to the Model

Analyst inputs to the model reflect metric adjustments to account for historical data anomalies, forward-looking performance shifts, or non-recurring events that may otherwise skew the time series.

The analyst used the standard state-wide county median for the calculation of missing education and health services personal income data. The analyst also entered 2024 data for population size and trend.

Long-Term Liability Burden

Long-Term Liability Burden - 'Strong'

Rocky Mount's liabilities to governmental revenue have deteriorated while carrying costs to governmental expenditure and liabilities to personal income remain moderately strong. The long-term liability composite metric in 2024 is at the 66th percentile, indicating a somewhat lower liability burden relative to the Fitch's local government rating portfolio.

-- Liabilities to personal income: 4.2% Analyst Input (61st percentile) (vs. 4.5% 2024 Actual)

-- Liabilities to governmental revenue: 132.2% Analyst Input (72nd percentile) (vs. 141.0% 2024 Actual)

-- Carrying costs to governmental expenditures: 12.7% (2024) (65th percentile)

The analyst adjusted net direct debt in the calculation of liabilities to personal income and liabilities to governmental revenues to reflect the amortization of debt taking place in fiscal 2025.

Dedicated Tax Security

The bonds are special obligations of the city payable from sales tax revenue and other state distributions. The Trust Estate includes proceeds of the Article 39 1% sales and use tax, Article 40 0.5% sales and use tax, Article 42 0.5% sales and use tax, and the hold-harmless distribution pursuant to Article 44 as well as franchise, telecommunications, cable television, beer and wine tax revenue and solid waste disposal tax revenue levied by the state and allocated to the city pursuant to a population-based or point of sale formula.

Dedicated Tax Key Rating Drivers

Growth Prospects for Revenues - 'aaa'

Fiscal 2024 pledged revenue totaled over $23.4 million, including $18.5 million from sales tax revenue and $5.0 million from other state distributions. Coverage of MADS is high at over 7x. Revenue grew faster than GDP over the past decade ending in 2024. Revenue has been on a positive trajectory since 2011, except for a mild 1.7% decline in 2013. Fitch expects revenue growth to continue at a pace above national economic performance and in line with a 'aaa' growth prospects assessment. Online retail sales were added in 2015, and maintenance and repair sales were added in March 2016.

Sensitivity and Resilience - 'aaa'

Based on the pledged revenue history, Fitch's Analytical Sensitivity Tool (FAST) generates less than a 1% decline in a moderate downturn scenario. The largest decline in historical revenue was equal to over 5% from fiscal 2008 to fiscal 2010, reflecting the impact of the great recession on the state's economy. Pledged revenue has since notably improved.

Based on fiscal 2024 revenue and coverage, the structure could tolerate at least an 86% drop before MADS coverage falls to 1.0x. This level of tolerance is equivalent to 17x the largest actual revenue decline in the review period. Fitch considers these results to be consistent with a 'aaa' assessment. Fitch expects coverage levels to remain relatively stable; the risk of additional leverage is remote as excess revenue is used to support city operations and there are no additional debt plans.

Exposure To Related Government

Fitch believes that the pledged revenue would not be insulated from the general operations of the city in the event of a bankruptcy filing. As such, the rating on the dedicated tax bonds is capped at the city's 'AA' IDR.

PROFILE

Rocky Mount is located one hour east of the city of Raleigh at the intersection of I95 and U.S. Highway 64, with access to employment centers like Research Triangle Park and major transportation hubs like Raleigh-Durham International Airport, both approximately one hour away. The city spans 44 miles within Nash and Edgecombe counties. The city's population in 2023 was 54,245.

Rocky Mount's major employment sectors are manufacturing, retail trade and healthcare. The manufacturing sector is led by Pfizer which is one of the city's top employers and taxpayers. Pfizer recently announced layoffs at its Rocky Mount facility, citing site modernization and changes in demand, though the move is expected to affect only about 60 of the company's more than 1,000 employees in the city.

Sources of Information

In addition to sources of information identified in Fitch's applicable criteria specified below, this action was informed by data from DIVER by Solve.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit https://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: GE Healthcare's $2.3 Billion Takeover Is a Bold Growth Play. Here's Why. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

GE Healthcare Technologies is trying to kick-start growth, which should please investors.

The medical imaging and diagnostic company announced on Thursday an agreement to acquire Intelerad, a medical imaging software provider with a presence in outpatient ambulatory care settings, for $2.3 billion in cash.

The deal should close in the first half of 2026.

The deal complements GE HealthCare's inpatient footprint. "The combined business will broaden customer reach and [deliver] a comprehensive, integrated imaging solution across multiple care settings," said the company in a news release. "In addition, Intelerad's business model accelerates GE HealthCare's shift towards a [software as a service], SaaS, model and significantly increases recurring revenue."

It's GE Healthcare's first acquisition since its separation from GE in January 2023, pointed out Citi analyst Joanne Wuensch in a Thursday report. "The transaction will be immediately accretive to top-line growth and adjusted [operating profit] margin, though slightly dilutive to adjusted earnings per share in the short term," she added. Wuensch rates GE Healthcare stock Hold and has an $83 price target for shares.

Revenue in the first full year of ownership should be about $270, said the company. Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, or Ebitda, margins are "in excess of 30%." GE Healthcare is expected to produce a 2025 Ebitda margin of almost 18% from sales of about $20.5 billion.

Investors don't seem to mind the dilution. Shares were up 0.4% in early trading at $75.02, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were up 0.4% and 0.3%, respectively. Friday's early gain for GE Healthcare stock followed a 3.4% jump on Thursday.

Coming into Friday trading, GE Healthcare's stock was down 4% so far this year and down 12% over the past 12 months.

Tariffs and trade concerns have weighed on investor sentiment. It's had an impact on Wall Street estimates. GE Healthcare is expected to make about $4.55 per share in 2025. That estimate was closer to $4.70 a year ago.

GE Healthcare earned about $4.50 in 2024, so earnings growth has been slow lately.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com
